---
title: 'Module #10 Hot Dogs'
author: "VaShay Carpenter"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
#Hotdogs Setup
hotdogs <- read.csv("http://datasets.flowingdata.com/hot-dog-contest-winners.csv")
colnames(hotdogs) <- c("Year", "Winner", "Eaten", "Country", "Record")
head(hotdogs)

#Hotdog_places barplot Setup
hotdog_places <- read_csv("http://datasets.flowingdata.com/hot-dog-places.csv")
hotdog_places <- as.matrix(hotdog_places) # Rename the columns to correspond to the years 2000-2010
colnames(hotdog_places) <- lapply(2000:2010, as.character)


#Econimics setup
head(economics)

year <- function(x) as.POSIXlt(x)$year + 1900
economics$year <- year(economics$date) # we setting up our analysis
head(economics)

#Our Weather ggplot2 setup
# 1. Pulling Data about Weather
weather_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vega/vega/master/docs/data/seattle-weather.csv" 

df <- read.csv(weather_url)

df$date <- as.Date(df$date)

df


```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
library("ggplot2")
library("gridExtra")
library("tidyverse")

#Hotdog: Barplot 
colors <- ifelse(hotdogs$Record == 1, "darkred", "grey")
barplot(hotdogs$Eaten, names.arg = hotdogs$Year, col=colors, border=NA,
main = "Nathan's Hot Dog Eating Contest Results, 1980-2010",  xlab="Year", ylab="Hot dogs and buns (HDBs) eaten")

#Hotdog: ggplot2
ggplot(hotdogs) + geom_bar(aes(x=Year, y=Eaten, fill=factor(Record)), stat="identity") + labs(title="Nathan's Hot Dog Eating Contest Results, 1980-2010", fill="New Record") + xlab("Year") + ylab("Hot dogs and buns (HDBs) eaten")

#Hotdog_places: Stacked Barplot
barplot(hotdog_places, border=NA, main="Hot Dog Eating Contest Results, 1980-2010", xlab="Year", ylab="Hot dogs and buns (HDBs) eaten")

#Qplots
plot1 <- qplot(date, unemploy / pop, data = economics, geom = "line")
plot1

plot2 <- qplot(date, uempmed, data = economics, geom = "line")
grid.arrange(plot1, plot2, ncol=2)
plot1 <- qplot(unemploy/pop, uempmed, data = economics, geom = c("point", "path"))
plot2 <- qplot(unemploy/pop, uempmed, data = economics, geom = c("point", "path"), color=year)
grid.arrange(plot1, plot2, ncol=2)
plot2

#Our Weather ggplot2 visualizations

# 2. Visualize the Weather Windows for Maximum and Minimum Temperatures in the first 60 entries of time series (Time Series of Temp Max vs Min)
ggplot(df[1:60, ], aes(x = date)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = temp_min, ymax = temp_max), fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(aes(y = temp_max, color = "Daytime Peak"), size = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = temp_min, color = "Nighttime Floor"), size = 1) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Daytime Peak" = "darkred", "Nighttime Floor" = "blue")) +
  labs(title = "Time Series: Thermal Variance & Survival Windows",
       x = "Deployment Date",
       y = "Temperature (C)",
       color = "Thermal Limit")

# 3. The Precipitation (Bar + Line Overlay)
ggplot(df[1:60, ], aes(x = date)) +
  geom_bar(aes(y = precipitation * 10), stat = "identity", fill = "grey", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(aes(y = wind), color = "orange", size = 1) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Environmental Friction: Wind vs. Precipitation",
       x = "Date",
       y = "Wind Speed (m/s) / Precip (Scaled)")
```
Blog Link: https://sizzleyezation.blogspot.com/2026/03/time-series-analysis-from-competitive.html
